original_hypothesis.title: YieldRegistry Rescue Function Enables Arbitrary Calls From Any Yield Account Draining User Staked Assets
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: YieldRegistry.rescue(yieldAccount, target, data) allows any POOL_ADMIN to execute arbitrary function calls from any YieldAccount via the registry's onlyRegistry modifier. Since YieldAccounts hold user staked assets (stETH, eETH, sDAI), a compromised or malicious pool admin can use rescue to transfer all staked assets out of every user's yield account. The function has no restrictions on target or data, and no timelock or multi-sig requirement at the contract level.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While POOL_ADMIN is a trusted role, the rescue function provides god-mode access to all user staked assets. The deployment scripts show POOL_ADMIN is initially set to the deployer address with no timelock.
deployment_assumptions: POOL_ADMIN key is secured; no timelock wraps the ACL role
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: DeployPoolFull.s.sol: aclManager.addPoolAdmin(deployer) grants deployer direct admin access

proof_of_signal.pattern: onlyPoolAdmin guard; YieldAccount(payable(yieldAccount)).rescue(target, data) executes arbitrary call from yield account

preconditions: POOL_ADMIN private key compromised or malicious admin action
Yield accounts hold staked assets (stETH, eETH, sDAI)
No external timelock or multi-sig protects the POOL_ADMIN role

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Arbitrary external call capability from user-controlled asset storage
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Complete admin discretion over all user yield positions
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - as long as yield accounts hold assets

operational_questions: Is the POOL_ADMIN role behind a timelock or multi-sig?
Can rescue be called on yield accounts that are actively staked?
Total value at risk across all yield accounts?
validation_requirements: Test rescue extracting stETH from an active yield position
Assess whether rescue bypasses any accounting checks

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call rescue for each yield account: rescue(account, stETH, transfer(attacker, balance))
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All user staked assets (stETH, eETH, sDAI) drained from yield accounts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users cannot repay debts (no yield to claim), creating cascading bad debt

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete drain of all user yield positions
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All yield staking strategies become insolvent
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Funds unrecoverable if transferred to external address
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All yield staking across Lido, EtherFi, and sDAI

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic - standard ERC20 transfer calls via rescue

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Rescue calls from YieldRegistry to yield accounts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Yield account balances dropping without corresponding unstake/repay

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
